elements.sent-2411-001: Gaze estimation encounters generalization challenges when dealing with out-of-distribution data.
elements.sent-2411-002: To address this problem, recent methods use neural radiance fields (NeRF) to generate augmented data.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing methods based on NeRF are computationally expensive and lack facial details.
elements.sent-2411-004: While 3DGS has been extensively examined in head avatars, it faces challenges with accurate gaze control and generalization across different subjects.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we propose GazeGaussian, a high-fidelity gaze redirection method that uses a two-stream 3DGS model to represent the face and eye regions separately.
elements.sent-2411-006: By leveraging the unstructured nature of 3DGS, we develop a novel eye representation for rigid eye rotation based on the target gaze direction.
elements.sent-2411-007: To enhance synthesis generalization across various subjects, we integrate an expression-conditional module to guide the neural renderer.
elements.sent-2411-008: Comprehensive experiments show that GazeGaussian outperforms existing methods in rendering speed, gaze redirection accuracy, and facial synthesis across multiple datasets.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also demonstrate that existing gaze estimation methods can leverage GazeGaussian to improve their generalization performance.
elements.sent-2411-010: The code will be available at: https://ucwxb.github.io/GazeGaussian/.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.90\linewidth]{fig/intro.pdf} \caption{GazeGaussian for gaze redirection: Given an input image, GazeGaussian deforms face and eye Gaussians from canonical space to generate high-fidelity head images with accurate gaze redirection.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-011: Gaze estimation is a fundamental component across various applications~\cite{andrist2014conversational, padmanaban2017optimizing, mavely2017eye , yet current estimators~\cite{cheng2024appearance, cheng2022gaze, xu2023learning often struggle to generalize effectively to out-of-distribution data.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: To address this, recent approaches~\cite{ruzzi2023gazenerf, yin2024Nerf_gaze, wang2023high have started exploring gaze redirection, which manipulates the gaze in an input image toward a target direction.}
elements.sent-2411-013: This process generates augmented data to enhance the generalization capabilities of gaze estimators.
elements.sent-2411-014: Earlier methods~\cite{ganin2016deepwarp, yu2020unsupervised, zhang2022gazeonce, zhang18_etra formulate gaze redirection as a 2D image manipulation task, relying on deep learning techniques to warp eye regions of the image toward the target gaze direction.}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, these 2D approaches overlook the inherently 3D nature of head and gaze manipulation, often resulting in poor spatial consistency and limited synthesis fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-016: With advancements in Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF)~\cite{nerf and its variants~\cite{wang2021neus, wei2024nto3d , several methods~\cite{zielonka2023instant, grassal2022neural, zheng2023pointavatar, hong2022headnerf have achieved 3D dynamic head representation and high-fidelity avatar synthesis.}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Meanwhile, to enable precise control of gaze direction, recent research~\cite{ruzzi2023gazenerf, yin2024Nerf_gaze, wang2023high has introduced approaches that decouple the face and eye regions, modeling each with separate neural fields to achieve accurate gaze redirection.}
elements.sent-2411-018: As NeRF-based methods are hindered by high computational demands, 3D Gaussian Splatting~\cite{kerbl20233d and its variants~\cite{lu2024scaffold, huang2024s3gaussian, wang2024plgs achieve impressive rendering quality with significantly faster training speeds.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Recent research~\cite{xiang2024flashavatar, qian2024gaussianavatars, xu2023gaussianheadavatar has applied these methods to 3D head animation, typically using face-tracking~\cite{tran2018nonlinear, zielonka2022towards parameters to model dynamic 3D head representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, existing 3DGS-based approaches neglect the accurate control of gaze direction and struggle to generalize across different subjects, limiting their effectiveness for gaze redirection.
elements.sent-2411-021: To address the above issues, we propose GazeGaussian, a high-fidelity gaze redirection method that leverages a two-stream 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model to represent the face and eye regions, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-022: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first integration of 3DGS into gaze redirection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-023: An overview is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:intro .}
elements.sent-2411-024: GazeGaussian begins by initializing the two-stream 3DGS model using a pre-trained neutral mesh on the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-025: This mesh is divided into distinct regions for the face and eyes.
elements.sent-2411-026: By employing gaze direction and face tracking codes, we optimize a deformation field for the face and a rotation field for the eyes, allowing us to adjust the neutral Gaussians accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-027: To achieve precise eye rotation aligned with the target gaze, we present a novel Gaussian Eye Rotation Representation (GERR).
elements.sent-2411-028: In contrast to methods like GazeNeRF that implicitly alter feature maps, GazeGaussian explicitly adjusts the position of Gaussians in the eye branch according to the desired gaze direction, utilizing the controllable nature of 3DGS.
elements.sent-2411-029: To address possible errors in gaze direction, GazeGaussian develops an eye rotation field to enhance redirection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-030: The two-stream Gaussians are rasterized into high-level features and sent to the neural renderer.
elements.sent-2411-031: Finally, to enhance synthesis generalization across different subjects and preserve facial details, we employ an expression-guided neural renderer (EGNR) to synthesize the final gaze-redirection images.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our main contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We introduce GazeGaussian, the first 3DGS-based gaze redirection pipeline, achieving precise gaze manipulation and high-fidelity head avatar synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-034: To enable rigid and accurate eye rotation based on the target gaze direction, we propose a novel two-stream 3DGS framework to decouple face and eye deformations, featuring a specialized Gaussian eye rotation for explicit control over eye movement.
elements.sent-2411-035: To enhance the synthesis generalization of 3DGS, we design an expression-guided neural renderer (EGNR) to retain facial details across various subjects.
elements.sent-2411-036: We conduct comprehensive experiments on ETH-XGaze, ColumbiaGaze, MPIIFaceGaze, and GazeCapture datasets, where GazeGaussian achieves state-of-the-art gaze redirection accuracy and facial synthesis quality with competitive rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-037: Gaze redirection is the task of manipulating the gaze direction of a face image to a target direction while preserving the subject's identity and other facial details.
elements.sent-2411-038: Earlier approaches for gaze redirection include novel view synthesis~\cite{criminisi2003gaze,kuster2012gaze,giger2014gaze , eye-replacement~\cite{qin2015eyegaze,shu2016eyeopener , and warping-based methods~\cite{ganin2016deepwarp,kononenko2015gaze, gazedirector .}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, these methods are limited by person-specific data requirements, restricted redirection range, and artifact introduction.
elements.sent-2411-040: To further improve gaze redirection, recent studies~\cite{park2019few, selflearning, he2019gazeredirection, xia2020controllable have employed neural network-based generative models.}
elements.sent-2411-041: STED~\cite{selflearning , building on the FAZE~\cite{park2019few , introduces a self-transforming encoder-decoder that generates full-face images with high-fidelity control over gaze direction and head pose.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Effective gaze redirection should account for both the 3D nature of eyeball rotation and the deformation of surrounding facial regions.
elements.sent-2411-043: With advancements in Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF)~\cite{nerf , several studies~\cite{li2022eyenerf, ruzzi2023gazenerf, yin2024Nerf_gaze, wang2023high have aimed to model the complex rotation of the eyeball.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: GazeNeRF~\cite{ruzzi2023gazenerf employs a two-stream MLP architecture to separately model the face only and eye regions, achieving improved gaze redirection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-045: However, these methods are hindered by substantial computational demands and limited rendering efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, gaze manipulation occurs at the feature map level and remains an implicit approach.
elements.sent-2411-047: In contrast, GazeGaussian allows for explicit control over eye rotations, improving gaze redirection accuracy and accelerating the synthesis process.. Head Avatar Synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-048: The synthesis of head avatars has garnered considerable attention in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-049: FLAME~\cite{li2017learning is a parameterized 3D head model that maps parameters of shape, expression, and pose onto 3D facial geometry, allowing for realistic and controllable head avatar generation.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Many subsequent works~\cite{VOCA2019,ranjan2018generating,fan2022faceformer,peng2023selftalk,peng2023emotalk,chen2023diffusiontalker focus on using the FLAME model for speech-driven head avatar animation.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Recent head animation techniques can be categorized into two main approaches: NeRF-based methods and 3DGS-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-052: NeRF-based approaches~\cite{gafni2021dynamic, zielonka2023instant, hong2022headnerf, zheng2023pointavatar leverage neural radiance fields to deform facial movements from a canonical space.}
elements.sent-2411-053: HeadNeRF~\cite{hong2022headnerf introduces a parametric head model that controls facial shape, expression, and albedo under different lighting conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-054: With the emergence of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)~\cite{kerbl20233d , serveral approaches~\cite{qian2024gaussianavatars, xiang2024flashavatar, xu2023gaussianheadavatar, dhamo2023headgas have explored its application in head avatar modeling.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Gaussian Head Avatar~\cite{xu2023gaussian initializes Gaussians with a neutral mesh head and incorporates MLPs to deform complex facial expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-056: While these methods produce impressive results in creating 3D head avatars, they overlook precise gaze control and do not generalize well across different subjects.
elements.sent-2411-057: In contrast, GazeGaussian emphasizes precise gaze direction control by decoupling facial animations and gaze movement within a two-stream model.
elements.sent-2411-058: Furthermore, we introduce an expression-guided neural renderer designed to improve the quality of synthesis.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.90\textwidth]{fig/pipeline.pdf} \caption{Pipeline of GazeGaussian. We initialize face-only and eye regions from a pre-trained neutral mesh. Using target expression codes, head rotation, and gaze direction, GazeGaussian optimizes face deformation and eye rotation fields to transform the neutral Gaussians. The transformed Gaussians are splatted into feature maps. The expression codes guide the neural renderer through cross-attention, enabling the rendering of feature maps into high-fidelity images, which are then supervised by multi-view RGB images.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-059: The pipeline of GazeGaussian is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline , including the two-stream Gaussians and the proposed expression-guided neural renderer.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Before the beginning of the pipeline, we follow the data preprocessing in GazeNeRF~\cite{ruzzi2023gazenerf and Gaussian Head Avatar~\cite{xu2023gaussianheadavatar , which include background removal, gaze direction normalization, and facial tracking for each frame.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: To obtain a neutral mesh for Gaussian initialization, we first reconstruct a Sign Distance Function (SDF) based neutral geometry and then optimize a face deformation field and an eye rotation field from the training data.
elements.sent-2411-062: A neutral mesh representing a coarse geometry across different subjects can be extracted using DMTet~\cite{shen2021deep .}
elements.sent-2411-063: We then partition the neutral mesh into face-only and eye regions using 3D landmarks, initializing the two-stream Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-064: Based on these neutral Gaussians, GazeGaussian optimizes a face deformation field and an eye rotation field to transform the Gaussians according to the target expression codes, gaze direction, and head rotation.
elements.sent-2411-065: Next, we concatenate the two-stream Gaussians and rasterize them into a high-dimensional feature map representing the head, face-only, and eye regions.
elements.sent-2411-066: Finally, these feature maps are fed into the expression-guided neural renderer to generate high-fidelity gaze redirection images.
elements.sent-2411-067: The ground truth image is used to supervise the rendered face-only, head, and eye images.
elements.sent-2411-068: The vanilla 3D Gaussians~\cite{kerbl20233d with $N$ points are represented by their positions $X$, the multi-channel color $C$, the rotation $Q$, scale $S$ and opacity $A$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The color $C$ is computed using spherical harmonics, and the rotation $Q$ is represented as the quaternion.
elements.sent-2411-070: These Gaussians are then rasterized and rendered to a multi-channel image $I$ based on the camera parameters $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-071: This rendering process can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:render} I = \mathcal{R}(X, C, Q, S, A; \mu),\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:render} I = R(X, C, Q, S, A; \mu),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: Our task is to synthesize a head avatar conditioned on gaze direction, head rotation, and expression latent codes.
elements.sent-2411-073: To decouple the complex movements in the face and eyes, we introduce a two-stream Gaussian model consisting of a face-only branch and an eye branch.
elements.sent-2411-074: In the following subsections, we will describe the face deformation and eye rotation processes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-075: For the face-only branch, inspired by Gaussian Head Avatar, we first construct canonical neutral face Gaussians with attributes: $\{\boldsymbol{X _0^f, \boldsymbol{F _0^f, \boldsymbol{Q _0^f, \boldsymbol{S _0^f, \boldsymbol{A _0^f\}$, which are fully optimizable.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The neutral color is directly predicted from the point-wise feature vectors $\boldsymbol{F _0^f$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Then we construct several MLPs, denoted as $\boldsymbol{\Phi ^f$, to serve as face deformation fields that transform the neutral face Gaussians.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Next, we describe the process of applying offsets to each Gaussian attribute.
elements.sent-2411-080: We predict the displacements respectively controlled by the latent codes and the head pose in the canonical space through two different MLPs: $_{def ^{exp}\mathcal{F ^f \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^f$ and $_{def ^{pose}\mathcal{F ^f \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^f$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Then, we add them to the neutral positions. \begin{equation}\begin{split} X^f = \boldsymbol{X}_0^f + \lambda_{exp}(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f) _{def}^{exp}\mathcal{F}^f(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f, \theta) + \lambda_{pose}(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f) _{def}^{pose}\mathcal{F}^f(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f, \beta), \end{split}\end{equation} $\theta$ denoting latent codes including expression and identity coefficients and $\beta$ denoting the head pose.
elements.sent-2411-082: Following the approach in Gaussian Head Avatar, the predefined hyperparameters are set as $t_1 = 0.15$ and $t_2 = 0.25$.
elements.sent-2411-084: Modeling dynamic details requires a color that varies with expressions.
elements.sent-2411-085: The color is directly predict by two color MLPs: $_{col ^{exp}\mathcal{F ^f \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^f$ and $_{col ^{pose}\mathcal{F ^f \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^f$: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:color} \begin{split} C^f = \lambda_{exp}(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f) _{col}^{exp}\mathcal{F}^f(\boldsymbol{F}_0^f, \theta) + \lambda_{pose}(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f) _{col}^{pose}\mathcal{F}^f(\boldsymbol{F}_0^f, \beta), \end{split}\end{equation} Rotation, Scale and Opacity $\{Q^f, S^f, A^f\}$ of the Gaussians.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: These three attributes are also dynamic, capturing detailed expression-related appearance changes.
elements.sent-2411-087: We just use another two attribute MLPs: $_{att ^{exp}\mathcal{F ^f \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^f$ and $_{att ^{pose}\mathcal{F ^f \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^f$ to predict their shift from the neutral value. \begin{equation}\begin{split} \{Q^f, S^f, A^f\} = \{\mathbf{Q}_0^f, \mathbf{S}_0^f, \mathbf{A}_0^f\} + \lambda_{exp}(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f) _{att}^{exp}\mathcal{F}^f(\boldsymbol{F}_0^f, \theta) + \lambda_{pose}(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f) _{att}^{pose}\mathcal{F}^f(\boldsymbol{F}_0^f, \beta), \end{split}\end{equation} Finally, we apply rigid rotations and translations to transform Gaussians in the canonical space to the world space.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Then, these Gaussians are rasterized into the feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-089: The above face-only branch can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{M}_f = \mathcal{R}(\{X^f, C^f, Q^f, S^f, A^f\}) = \mathcal{R}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^f(\boldsymbol{X}_0^f, \boldsymbol{F}_0^f, \boldsymbol{Q}_0^f, \boldsymbol{S}_0^f, \boldsymbol{A}_0^f; \theta, \beta)), \end{split}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{R}$ represents the rasterizer and $\mathcal{M _f$ indicates the feature map from the face-only branch.}

elements.sent-2411-090: For the eye branch, we also construct canonical neutral eye Gaussians with attributes $\{\boldsymbol{X _0^e, \boldsymbol{F _0^e, \boldsymbol{Q _0^e, \boldsymbol{S _0^e, \boldsymbol{A _0^e\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: These attributes share the same dimensionality as those in the face-only branch, except that $\boldsymbol{S _0^e \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times 1}$ is constrained to be spherical, aligning with the rotational properties of the eyeball.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Directly applying the same deformation strategy as for the face branch would fail to fully leverage the unique characteristics of eyeball rotational motion, resulting in insufficient gaze redirection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-094: Therefore, we first rotate the eye Gaussians in the canonical space and then incorporate the eye geometry information from the latent codes of different subjects to generate biases.
elements.sent-2411-095: Since the gaze labels may contain noise, directly using the normalized gaze direction $\varphi$ to rotate the Gaussians would lead to numerical optimization errors.
elements.sent-2411-096: To address this, we optimize two separate MLPs: $_{rot ^{gaze}\mathcal{F ^e \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^e$ and $_{def ^{exp}\mathcal{F ^e \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^e$ to predict the biases for Gaussian rotation and displacement. \begin{equation}\begin{split} X^e = _{def}^{exp}\mathcal{F}^f(\boldsymbol{X}_0^e, \theta) + _{rot}^{gaze}\mathcal{F}^e(\boldsymbol{X}_0^e, \varphi) \boldsymbol{X}_0^e, \end{split}\end{equation} Since eyes are relatively small and mainly influenced by the gaze direction, $\lambda$ used in the face is omitted here.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-099: We use two MLPs: $_{col ^{exp}\mathcal{F ^e \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^e$ and $_{col ^{gaze}\mathcal{F ^e \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^e$ to predict it: \begin{equation}C^e = _{att}^{exp}\mathcal{F}^e(\boldsymbol{F}_0^e, \theta) + _{col}^{gaze}\mathcal{F}^e (\boldsymbol{X}_0^e, \varphi),\end{equation} Rotation, Scale and Opacity $\{Q^e, S^e, A^e\}$ of the Gaussians.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: We just use another two attribute MLPs $_{att ^{exp}\mathcal{F ^e \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^e$ and $_{att ^{gaze}\mathcal{F ^e \in \boldsymbol{\Phi ^e$ to predict their shift. \begin{equation}\begin{split} \{Q^e, S^e, A^e\} = \{\mathbf{Q}_0^e, \mathbf{S}_0^e, \mathbf{A}_0^e\} + _{att}^{exp}\mathcal{F}^e(\boldsymbol{F}_0^e, \theta) + _{att}^{gaze}\mathcal{F}^e(\boldsymbol{F}_0^e, \varphi), \end{split}\end{equation} Finally, we transform Gaussians in the canonical space to the world space.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Then these eye Gaussians are rasterized into the feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-102: The eye branch is formulated as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{M}_e = \mathcal{R}(\{X^e, C^e, Q^e, S^e, A^e\}) = \mathcal{R}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^e(\boldsymbol{X}_0^e, \boldsymbol{F}_0^e, \boldsymbol{Q}_0^e, \boldsymbol{S}_0^e, \boldsymbol{A}_0^e; \theta, \varphi)), \end{split}\end{equation} To obtain the full head rendering, we simply concat the two-stream Gaussians and rasterized them into feature maps: \begin{equation}M_h = R(\{X^{f}, C^{f}, Q^{f}, S^{f}, A^{f}\} \nonumber \{X^{e}, C^{e}, Q^{e}, S^{e}, A^{e}\}),\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-103: After obtaining the rasterized feature maps from Gaussians, a UNet-like neural renderer $\mathbf{R}$ opts to synthesize the final face-only, eyes, and head images $\{\mathcal{I _f, \mathcal{I _e, \mathcal{I _h\}$: \begin{equation}\{\mathcal{I}_f, \mathcal{I}_e, \mathcal{I}_h\} = \mathbf{R}(\{\mathcal{M}_f, \mathcal{M}_e, \mathcal{M}_h\}),\end{equation} To enhance the generalization ability across different subjects, we inject the latent codes \(\theta\) into the neural renderer through a slice cross-attention module.}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: Let \(\boldsymbol{F _b\) represent the bottleneck feature obtained from the encoder of \(\mathbf{R}\).}
elements.sent-2411-105: We utilize the latent codes to query this bottleneck feature, using it as a conditional signal to guide the renderer's synthesis process.
elements.sent-2411-106: The guiding process can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{F}'_b = \boldsymbol{F}_b + \boldsymbol{F}_b \cdot \text{Attn}(q=\theta, k=\boldsymbol{F}_b, v=\boldsymbol{F}_b)),\end{equation} where \(\text{Attn (\cdot)\) denotes the cross-attention operation that fuses the latent codes with the bottleneck feature.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Then the refined feature \(\boldsymbol{F '_b\) is decoded as final images.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Initialization for the 3D Gaussians (3DGS) is crucial for stable optimization.
elements.sent-2411-109: Following Gaussian Head Avatar, we initialize the two-stream Gaussians using the neutral mesh extracted from an SDF field.
elements.sent-2411-110: This neutral mesh provides a coarse geometry and texture, which are used to initialize the positions and features of the Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-111: To decouple the face-only and eye regions, we compute the 3D neutral landmarks and use learnable parameters to define the vertices near the eyes as the initial Gaussians for the eye region, while the rest of the head is used to initialize the face-only Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-112: Additionally, we transfer the parameters of all deformation and color MLPs while the MLPs for attribute prediction and the expression-guided neural renderer are randomly initialized.. Image Synthesis Loss.
elements.sent-2411-113: The masked ground truth image $\mathcal{I _{gt}$ is used to supervise the rendered images ${\mathcal{I _f, \mathcal{I _e, \mathcal{I _h}$, corresponding to the face-only, eyes, and head regions, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Additionally, we enforce the first three channels of the feature maps ${\mathcal{M _f, \mathcal{M _e, \mathcal{M _h}$ to learn the RGB colors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: For each rendered image and its corresponding feature map, we apply the same loss functions.
elements.sent-2411-116: Taking the rendered eye image as an example, we mask the ground truth image using an eye mask and then apply L1 loss, SSIM loss, and LPIPS loss on the masked image: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{I}}^e = ||\mathcal{I}_{gt}-\mathcal{I}_e||_1 + \lambda_{SSIM}(1 - SSIM(\mathcal{I}_{gt}, \mathcal{I}_e)) + \lambda_{VGG}VGG(\mathcal{I}_{gt}, \mathcal{I}_e), \end{split}\end{equation} where $\lambda_{SSIM =\lambda_{VGG =0.1$ is the weight of loss.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: The image synthesis loss is the sum of the three renderer images and three feature maps: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{I}} = \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{I}}^f + \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{I}}^e + \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{I}}^h + \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{M}}^f + \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{M}}^e + \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{M}}^h, \end{split}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{L _{\mathcal{I ^f, \mathcal{L _{\mathcal{I ^e, \mathcal{L _{\mathcal{I ^h$ represent the losses for the rendered face-only, eye, and head images, respectively.}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation} L_{I} = L_{I}^f + L_{I}^e + L_{I}^h + L_{M}^f + L_{M}^e + L_{M}^h, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-118: The image synthesis loss ensures the full disentanglement of the eye and the rest of the face.. Gaze Redirection Loss.
elements.sent-2411-119: To improve task-specific performance and eliminate task-relevant inconsistencies between the target image $\mathcal{I _{gt}$ and the reconstructed head image $\mathcal{I _{h}$, we adopt the functional loss used in STED~\cite{selflearning and GazeNeRF~\cite{ruzzi2023gazenerf .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The gaze redirection loss can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{G}} (\mathcal{I}_{h}, \mathcal{I}_{gt}) = \mathcal{E}_{\text{ang}}(\psi^g(\mathcal{I}_{wf}), \psi^g(\mathcal{I}_{gt})) \mathcal{E}_{\text{ang}}(\mathbf{v}, \ \hat{\mathbf{v}}) = \arccos \frac{\mathbf{v} \cdot {\hat{\mathbf{v}}}}{\|\mathbf{v}\| \, \|{\hat{\mathbf{v}}}\|} \, , \end{split}\end{equation} where $\psi^g(\cdot)$ represents the gaze direction estimated by a pre-trained gaze estimator network, and $\mathcal{E _{\text{ang (\cdot,\cdot)$ represents the angular error function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Our final loss function is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}= \lambda_{\mathcal{I}} \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{I}} + \lambda_{\mathcal{G}} \mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{G}},\end{equation} where $\lambda_{\mathcal{I =1.0$ and $\lambda_{\mathcal{G =0.1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: GazeGaussian is trained with the final loss until convergence.

elements.sent-2411-140: Following the experimental setup of GazeNeRF, we perform a within-dataset evaluation to compare the performance of GazeGaussian with other state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-141: All models are trained using 14.4K images derived from 10 frames per subject, with 18 camera view images per frame, covering 80 subjects in the ETH-XGaze training set.
elements.sent-2411-142: The evaluation is conducted on the person-specific test set of the ETH-XGaze dataset.
elements.sent-2411-143: This test set consists of 15 subjects, each with 200 images annotated with gaze and head pose labels.
elements.sent-2411-144: We follow the pairing setting in GazeNeRF, which pairs these 200 labeled images per subject as input and target samples, and the same pairings are used across all models to ensure fairness.
elements.sent-2411-145: Tab.~\ref{tab:comare_xgaze presents the quantitative results of GazeGaussian alongside baseline methods.}
elements.sent-2411-146: It can be observed that GazeGaussian consistently outperforms prior methods across all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-147: Specifically, our approach achieves the lowest errors in both gaze and head redirection ($6.622^\circ$ and $2.128^\circ$, respectively), demonstrating its superior precision in gaze and head control.
elements.sent-2411-148: Compared to the previous SOTA method GazeNeRF, which applies rotation to feature map for gaze redirection, GazeGaussian adopts a Gaussian eye rotation to explicitly control eye movement.
elements.sent-2411-149: Such a technique not only improves redirection accuracy but also significantly boosts rendering quality.
elements.sent-2411-150: Additionally, GazeGaussian achieves a rendering speed of 74 FPS, nearly doubling the performance of GazeNeRF, underscoring its efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-151: In contrast, Gaussian Head Avatar (GHA), the latest model built on Gaussian-based representations, struggles to deliver competitive performance in gaze and head redirection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-152: The lack of dedicated mechanisms for gaze disentanglement and explicit eye region modeling in GHA leads to poor performance.
elements.sent-2411-153: By decoupling the face and eye representation with two-stream Gaussians, GazeGaussian offers both higher accuracy and better visual quality, particularly in challenging scenarios involving extreme head poses or subtle gaze variations.
elements.sent-2411-154: We present a qualitative comparison of different methods in Fig.~\ref{fig:visualization .}
elements.sent-2411-155: GHA struggles to preserve personal identity in the generated face images, which is quantitatively verified as the low `identity similarity' in Tab.~\ref{tab:comare_xgaze .}
elements.sent-2411-156: Moreover, GHA produces blurred and unrealistic eye regions, significantly degrading the visual quality of gaze redirection.
elements.sent-2411-157: GazeNeRF, which implicitly rotates the feature map, fails to effectively control eye appearance under extreme gaze directions (as shown in the last row).
elements.sent-2411-158: Furthermore, it struggles with rendering fine-grained facial details and exhibits notable artifacts in hair rendering, particularly in the last two rows.
elements.sent-2411-159: Overall, the inability to accurately handle eye details in both GHA and GazeNeRF limits their effectiveness in gaze redirection.
elements.sent-2411-160: In contrast, GazeGaussian consistently produces highly realistic results, even under challenging conditions, setting a new benchmark for gaze redirection tasks.

elements.sent-2411-189: We present GazeGaussian, a high-fidelity gaze redirection pipeline that uses a two-stream model to represent face and eye regions separately.
elements.sent-2411-190: We present a new Gaussian-based representation of the eye to accurately depict eye rotations, along with an expression-conditional neural renderer that enhances the fidelity of gaze redirection.
elements.sent-2411-191: Numerous experiments have shown that GazeGaussian achieves state-of-the-art performance on the task of gaze direction, paving the way for more robust gaze estimation in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-192: In this work, we propose GazeGaussian, the first high-fidelity gaze redirection method that uses a two-stream 3DGS model to represent the face and eye regions separately.
elements.sent-2411-193: Leveraging the unstructured nature of 3DGS, we develop a novel representation of the eye for rigid eye rotation based on the target gaze direction.
elements.sent-2411-194: To enable synthesis generalization across various subjects, we integrate an expression-guided module to inject subject-specific information into the neural renderer.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.90\linewidth]{fig/intro.pdf} \caption{Gaze redirection: Given an input image, GazeGaussian deforms face and eye Gaussians from canonical space to generate high-fidelity head images with accurate gaze redirection.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-196: To our knowledge, this is the first integration of 3DGS into gaze redirection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-197: To achieve precise eye rotation aligned with the target gaze, we present a novel Gaussian Eye Rotation Representation.
elements.sent-2411-198: Finally, to enable the generalization ability across different subjects and inject subject-specific information, we employ an expression-guided neural renderer (EGNR) to synthesize the final gaze-redirection images.
elements.sent-2411-199: We introduce GazeGaussian, the first 3DGS-based gaze redirection pipeline, achieving precise gaze manipulation and generalizable head avatar synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-200: To equip 3DGS with generalization ability, we design an expression-guided neural renderer (EGNR) to integrate subject-specific characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-201: However, these methods are limited by person-specific data, restricted redirection range, and artifact introduction.
elements.sent-2411-202: STED~\cite{selflearning , building on FAZE~\cite{park2019few , introduces a self-transforming encoder-decoder that generates full-face gaze redirection images.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: GazeNeRF~\cite{ruzzi2023gazenerf employs a two-stream MLP architecture to separately model the face and eye regions, achieving improved gaze redirection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-204: Gaze manipulation occurs at the feature maps and remains an implicit approach.
elements.sent-2411-205: FLAME~\cite{li2017learning is a parameterized 3D head model that maps parameters of shape, expression, and pose onto 3D facial geometry, allowing for controllable head avatar generation.}
elements.sent-2411-206: With the emergence of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)~\cite{kerbl20233d , several approaches~\cite{qian2024gaussianavatars, xiang2024flashavatar, xu2023gaussianheadavatar, dhamo2023headgas have explored its application in single head avatar modeling.}}
elements.sent-2411-207: Gaussian Head Avatar~\cite{xu2023gaussianheadavatar initializes Gaussians with a neutral mesh head and incorporates MLPs to deform complex facial expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-208: However, these methods ignore precise gaze control and are confined to the single avatar animation.
elements.sent-2411-209: In contrast, GazeGaussian achieves precise gaze direction control by decoupling facial animations and gaze movement within a two-stream model.
elements.sent-2411-210: Furthermore, an expression-guided neural renderer is designed to inject person-specific information for generalization ability across different subjects.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.90\textwidth]{fig/pipeline.pdf} \caption{Pipeline of GazeGaussian. We initialize face-only and eye regions from a pre-trained neutral mesh. Using target expression codes, head rotation, and gaze direction, GazeGaussian optimizes face deformation and eye rotation fields to transform the neutral Gaussians. The transformed Gaussians are splatted into feature maps. The expression codes guide the neural renderer through cross-attention, enabling the rendering of feature maps into high-fidelity gaze redirection images, which are then supervised by multi-view ground truths.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-211: The vanilla 3D Gaussians~\cite{kerbl20233d with $n$ points are represented by their center $\mu \in \mathbb{R ^3$, the color computed by $k$ degrees of spherical harmonics $c \in \mathbb{R ^{3(k+1)^2}$, the quaternion rotation $R \in \mathbb{R ^{4 $, scale $S \in \mathbb{R ^{3 $ and opacity $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ can be obtained by $\Sigma = RSS^{T R^{T}$, which then formulates the Gaussian function $G(x) = e^{- \frac{1 {2 (x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1 (x-\mu)}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: These Gaussians are then rasterized into an image $I$, in which the pixel color $C$ is computed by blending $N$ ordered Gaussians overlapping the pixel: \begin{equation}C = \sum^N_{i=1}{c_i \alpha_i \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}}(1-\alpha_j),\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-214: To decouple the complex movements in the face and eyes, we introduce a two-stream Gaussian model consisting of a face-only deformation branch and an eye rotation branch in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-215: For the face-only branch, inspired by Gaussian Head Avatar~\cite{xu2023gaussianheadavatar , we first construct canonical neutral face Gaussians with trainable attributes: $\{\mu_0^f, z_0^f, R_0^f, S_0^f, \alpha_0^f\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-216: All attributes are identical to a standard Gaussian, except for $z_0^f \in \mathbb{R ^{128}$, which signifies the point-wise feature vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Rather than directly modeling complex color $c^f$, we decode $z_0^f$ into $c^f$ and affect other attributes' deformation to capture intricate facial details.
elements.sent-2411-218: To transform canonical face Gaussians into the target space, we first model the influence of latent codes $\tau$ and head pose $\gamma$ through respective weights $\lambda_\tau$ and $\lambda_\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-219: We compute the minimum distance $d$ from each Gaussian center $\mu$ to the 3D facial landmarks (excluding eye regions).
elements.sent-2411-220: Based on the distance, we assign influence weights $\lambda_\tau$ and $\lambda_\gamma$ to the expressive latent codes $\tau$ and head pose $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-221: For Gaussians near landmarks ($d < d_1$), they are primarily influenced by expressive latent codes and set $\lambda_\tau = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-222: For distant Gaussians ($d > d_2$), they are predominantly affected by head pose, with $\lambda_\tau = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-223: To ensure a smooth transition between these regions ($d_2 \geq d \geq d_1$), we define $\lambda_\tau = (d_2 - d)/(d_2 - d_1)$.
elements.sent-2411-224: Consequently, the pose weight is determined as $\lambda_\gamma = 1 - \lambda_\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-225: The distance thresholds are empirically set to $d_1 = 0.15$ and $d_2 = 0.25$.
elements.sent-2411-226: After that, we develop MLPs $\{E_\mu^f, E_c^f, E_\kappa^f\} \in E^f$ for latent codes-conditioned expression and $\{P_\mu^f, P_c^f, P_\kappa^f\} \in P^f$ for head pose-conditioned deformation: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mu^f = \mu_0^f + \lambda_\tau E_\mu^f(\mu_0^f, \tau) + \lambda_\gamma P_\mu^f(\mu_0^f, \gamma), c^f = \lambda_\tau E_c^f(z_0^f, \tau) + \lambda_\gamma P_c^f(z_0^f, \gamma), \kappa^f = \kappa_0^f + \lambda_\tau E_\kappa^f(z_0^f, \tau) +\lambda_\gamma P_\kappa^f(z_0^f, \gamma), \end{split}\end{equation} where $\kappa^f=\{R^f, S^f, \alpha^f\}$ and $\kappa_0^f=\{R_0^f, S_0^f, \alpha_0^f\}$.
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation} \mu^f = \mu_0^f + \lambda_\tau E_\mu^f(\mu_0^f, \tau) + \lambda_\gamma P_\mu^f(\mu_0^f, \gamma), c^f = \lambda_\tau E_c^f(z_0^f, \tau) + \lambda_\gamma P_c^f(z_0^f, \gamma), \kappa^f = \kappa_0^f + \lambda_\tau E_\kappa^f(z_0^f, \tau) +\lambda_\gamma P_\kappa^f(z_0^f, \gamma), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-227: Finally, we apply rigid rotations and translations to transform Gaussians in the canonical space to the world space.
elements.sent-2411-228: Then, these Gaussians are rasterized into the feature maps: \begin{equation}\mathcal{M}_f = \mathcal{R}(\{\mu^f, c^f, R^f, S^f, \alpha^f\}),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{R}$ represents the rasterizer and $\mathcal{M _f$ indicates the feature map from the face-only branch.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Different from Gaussian Head Avatar~\cite{xu2023gaussianheadavatar , we design an eye branch for accurate eye modeling and gaze redirection.}
elements.sent-2411-230: For the eye branch, we construct canonical neutral eye Gaussians with attributes $\{\mu_0^e, z_0^e, R_0^e, S_0^e, \alpha_0^e\}$.
elements.sent-2411-231: These attributes share the same dimension as those in the face-only branch, except that $S_0^e \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times 1}$ is constrained to be spherical, aligning with the rotational properties of the eyeball.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Since eyes are relatively small and mainly influenced by the gaze direction, $\lambda$ used in the face is omitted here.
elements.sent-2411-233: Directly applying the same face deformation strategy fails to fully leverage the unique characteristics of eyeball rotational motion, resulting in inaccurate gaze redirection.
elements.sent-2411-234: Therefore, we first rotate the eye Gaussians in the canonical space and then incorporate the expressive latent codes $\tau$ to generate deformation offsets.
elements.sent-2411-235: Since the gaze labels contain noise, directly using the normalized gaze direction $\varphi$ to rotate the Gaussians would lead to numerical optimization errors.
elements.sent-2411-236: To address this, we optimize two separate MLPs: $E_\mu^e \in E^e$ and $G_\mu^e \in G^e$ to predict the biases for Gaussian eye rotation. \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mu^e = E_\mu^e(\mu_0^e, \tau) + G_\mu^e(\mu_0^e, \varphi) \mu_0^e, \end{split}\end{equation} Other attributes are deformed in a similar way with $\{E_c^e,E_a^e\} \in E^e$ and $\{G_c^e,G_a^e\} \in G^e$: \begin{equation}\begin{split} c^e = E_c^e(z_0^e, \tau) + G_c^e(z_0^e, \varphi), \kappa^e = \kappa_0^e + E_\kappa^e(z_0^e, \tau) + G_\kappa^e(z_0^e, \varphi), \end{split}\end{equation} where $\kappa^e=\{R^e, S^e, \alpha^e\}$ and $\kappa_0^e=\{R_0^e, S_0^e, \alpha_0^e\}$.
elements.sent-2411-237: By the proposed explicit eye rotation, GazeGaussian succeeds in face and eye disentanglement, which is ignored in the Gaussian Head Avatar~\cite{xu2023gaussianheadavatar .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Finally, eye Gaussians are transformed into the world space.
elements.sent-2411-239: Then these eye Gaussians are rasterized into the feature maps: \begin{equation}\mathcal{M}_e = \mathcal{R}(\{\mu^e, c^e, R^e, S^e, \alpha^e\}),\end{equation} To obtain the full head rendering, we simply concat the two-stream Gaussians $\{\mu^{h , c^{h , R^{h , S^{h , \alpha^{h}\}=\text{Concat (\{\mu^f, c^f, R^f, S^f, \alpha^f\}, \{\mu^e, c^e, R^e, S^e, \alpha^e\})$ and rasterized them into head feature maps: \begin{equation}M_h = R(\{\mu^{h}, c^{h}, R^{h}, S^{h}, \alpha^{h}\}),\end{equation}.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-371: Compared to the baseline, GazeGaussian achieves high-fidelity gaze redirection with superior image synthesis quality.
elements.sent-2411-372: Our approach allows for the creation of lifelike portrait videos that may be exploited to spread misinformation, sway public opinion, and erode trust in media, with grave societal impacts.
elements.sent-2411-373: Thus, developing trustworthy techniques to discern real from fake content is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-374: We firmly oppose any unauthorized or harmful use of this technology and highlight the need to address ethical issues in its implementation.
elements.sent-2411-375: While GazeGaussian represents a significant advancement in gaze redirection quality, there is still one unresolved issue.
